meta.title: SF-SPSP-5: Snowpark Procedures, Functions, and Deployment

questions.stem: A Python UDF requires a third-party library that is not available in Snowflake's Anaconda channel. How do you make it available to the UDF?

options.text: Use a Snowpark Container Service instead of a UDF for unsupported libraries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Container Services are an option for complex workloads, but UDFs support custom imports via stage files — a Container Service is not required.

options.text: Install the package on the Snowflake server via SQL INSTALL PACKAGE command
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no `INSTALL PACKAGE` SQL command in Snowflake; packages must be declared at UDF creation time.

options.text: Upload the library as a zip file to a Snowflake stage and reference it in the UDF's IMPORTS clause
options.explanation: is correct. For Python packages not in Snowflake's Anaconda integration, you can package the library as a zip file, upload it to a named Snowflake stage (internal or external), and reference it using the IMPORTS clause (or the `imports` parameter in the Snowpark Python UDF decorator). Snowflake extracts the package at UDF execution time.

options.text: The package will be resolved automatically from PyPI when the UDF first executes
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not resolve packages from PyPI at runtime; only Anaconda channel packages or stage-imported packages are supported.

questions.stem: You deploy a Snowpark stored procedure using `is_permanent=True, stage_location='@my_stage'`. What does this configuration do?

options.text: The procedure code is stored in the Snowflake Procedure metadata catalog permanently
options.explanation: is incorrect. The metadata catalog stores only the procedure definition (name, signature, IMPORTS path), not the handler code itself.

options.text: The Python handler code and dependencies are uploaded to @my_stage; Snowflake stores the procedure definition in the catalog with a reference to the stage path
options.explanation: is correct. When creating a permanent Snowpark stored procedure (or UDF) with `stage_location`, Snowpark automatically uploads the serialized Python handler (.py file) and any specified packages to the designated Snowflake stage. The CREATE PROCEDURE DDL is registered in Snowflake's metadata catalog with an IMPORTS reference to the handler on the stage. The stage stores the actual handler code; the catalog stores the procedure definition.

options.text: The procedure persists across schema changes without redeployment
options.explanation: is incorrect. `is_permanent=True` controls persistence of the object registration, not resilience to schema changes.

options.text: The procedure is scheduled to run permanently at a fixed interval
options.explanation: is incorrect. Scheduling is handled by Snowflake Tasks, not the `is_permanent` flag.

questions.stem: You must distribute hyperparameter tuning for a Snowpark ML model across multiple Snowflake warehouse nodes simultaneously. Which Snowpark ML feature enables this?

options.text: Snowpark ML distributed GridSearchCV that parallelizes hyperparameter trials across Snowflake compute nodes
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark ML includes a distributed GridSearchCV implementation that parallelizes hyperparameter trials across Snowflake compute nodes. This allows testing many hyperparameter combinations simultaneously without external ML orchestration. The distributed API mirrors scikit-learn's GridSearchCV interface.

options.text: Snowpark ML does not support distributed hyperparameter tuning; use an external ML platform
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark ML does support distributed hyperparameter tuning via the distributed GridSearchCV API.

options.text: Snowpark ML distributes trials automatically when the warehouse is multi-cluster
options.explanation: is incorrect. The distributed training requires explicit use of the Snowpark ML distributed API, not just a multi-cluster warehouse configuration.

options.text: Use @udf(packages=['scikit-learn']) applied over a hyperparameter grid DataFrame
options.explanation: is incorrect. Applying a UDF over a grid DataFrame is not the supported distributed hyperparameter tuning approach in Snowpark ML.

questions.stem: You must pivot a Snowpark DataFrame that has columns [product, month, sales] into a wide format with months as columns (Jan, Feb, Mar, ...) and products as rows. Which Snowpark method supports this?

options.text: df.pivot(index='product', columns='month', values='sales')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.pivot()` with pandas-style keyword arguments is not the Snowpark API; the correct idiom chains `.groupBy().pivot().agg()`.

options.text: df.groupBy('product').pivot('month').agg(sum('sales'))
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark's pivot transformation uses a `groupBy().pivot().agg()` chain, mirroring Snowflake SQL's PIVOT syntax. `df.groupBy('product').pivot('month').agg(sum(col('sales')))` generates a wide-format DataFrame with one column per distinct month value.

options.text: df.groupBy('product').agg({m: sum('sales') for m in months})
options.explanation: is incorrect. This creates separate named aggregate columns for each month but does not perform a dynamic pivot.

options.text: df.unpivot('month', 'sales', ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar'])
options.explanation: is incorrect. `unpivot` converts wide format to long format — the reverse of what is needed.

questions.stem: You need a Snowpark function that takes a customer_id and returns multiple rows (all orders for that customer) from the ORDERS table. Which Snowflake function type is appropriate?

options.text: Scalar UDF returning an ARRAY type
options.explanation: is incorrect. A scalar UDF returns one value per row. An ARRAY packs multiple values into a single cell, not multiple rows.

options.text: Aggregate UDF (UDAF)
options.explanation: is incorrect. An aggregate UDF (UDAF) takes multiple rows as input and returns a single aggregate value — the opposite of what is needed.

options.text: Stored procedure with RESULTSET output
options.explanation: is incorrect. A stored procedure cannot be called inline in a FROM clause and is not used for row-generating functions in queries.

options.text: User-Defined Table Function (UDTF)
options.explanation: is correct. A User-Defined Table Function (UDTF) is designed to return a set of rows (a table) for each input row or call. In SQL, a UDTF is called in the FROM clause using `TABLE(my_udtf(customer_id))`. This is the correct type when you need to generate or return multiple rows per input.

questions.stem: A stored procedure must process files from a Snowflake internal stage, validate each file's schema, and load valid files into a table while logging invalid files to an error table. Which Snowflake feature allows listing and processing stage files within a stored procedure?

options.text: session.stage.list('@my_stage') returns a DataFrame of files in the stage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark does not have a `session.stage.list()` method.

options.text: session.file.list('@my_stage') returns a list of stage file paths
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark does not have a `session.file.list()` method for enumerating stage files.

options.text: session.read.option('pattern', '.*').csv('@my_stage') automatically discovers and lists files
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.read.csv()` reads file content as a DataFrame but does not provide a listing of stage files with metadata for validation logic.

options.text: session.sql('LIST @my_stage').collect() returns file metadata rows that can be iterated
options.explanation: is correct. `session.sql('LIST @my_stage').collect()` executes the Snowflake LIST command, which returns metadata about files in a stage (name, size, md5, last_modified). The result rows can be iterated in Python to process each file individually.

questions.stem: You are deploying a Snowpark Python data pipeline to production. The pipeline has multiple Python files and a requirements.txt with 15 packages. What is the recommended project structure and deployment approach?

options.text: Use a Makefile with custom boto3 scripts to upload to S3 and reference from Snowflake external stage
options.explanation: is incorrect. Using boto3 to upload to S3 and referencing an external stage is a valid workaround but adds unnecessary complexity compared to the Snowflake CLI.

options.text: Copy all Python files into a single module and inline all imports
options.explanation: is incorrect. Consolidating all code into a single file is not a sustainable practice for multi-file pipelines and is not the recommended approach.

options.text: Manually zip all Python files, upload to a stage with PUT, and register stored procedures with IMPORTS
options.explanation: is incorrect. Manual zipping and PUT is a valid but lower-level approach that the Snowflake CLI automates and improves upon.

options.text: Use a Snowflake CLI (snow) project with snowflake.yml; deploy using 'snow snowpark deploy' which packages code and uploads to a stage
options.explanation: is correct. The Snowflake CLI (`snow`) provides native support for Snowpark projects. With a project configuration file (`snowflake.yml`), the `snow snowpark deploy` command automatically packages Python source files, resolves package dependencies, uploads to the configured stage, and registers stored procedures and UDFs with correct IMPORTS and PACKAGES references. This is the recommended CI/CD approach for Snowpark deployments.

questions.stem: A Python UDF is registered using `@udf(packages=['pandas', 'numpy'])`. What does the `packages` parameter specify?

options.text: Python packages to be installed on the client machine before the UDF runs
options.explanation: is incorrect. The packages parameter specifies server-side dependencies, not client-side packages.

options.text: Python packages to be bundled into the UDF's zip archive
options.explanation: is incorrect. The packages are not bundled into a zip archive; they are resolved from the Anaconda channel at runtime.

options.text: Python packages to be imported in the UDF handler function's local scope
options.explanation: is incorrect. The packages are made available in the server-side execution environment, not injected into local scope via imports.

options.text: Python packages available from Snowflake's Anaconda channel to be made available to the UDF's execution environment on Snowflake servers
options.explanation: is correct. The `packages` parameter in `@udf()` (and in `CREATE FUNCTION ... PACKAGES`) specifies Python packages to be pulled from Snowflake's curated Anaconda channel and made available in the UDF's runtime environment on Snowflake's servers. When the UDF executes, the listed packages are available for import. Packages must be in Snowflake's Anaconda channel or uploaded to a stage via IMPORTS.

questions.stem: A Snowpark stored procedure updates a table and should only commit if all updates succeed. If any update fails, all changes must be rolled back. Which Snowpark approach implements this?

options.text: Use session.sql('BEGIN TRANSACTION') and session.sql('COMMIT') explicitly
options.explanation: is incorrect. BEGIN + COMMIT alone does not handle the rollback case when an error occurs; you also need except-clause rollback logic.

options.text: Disable autocommit on the session so changes buffer until session.commit() is called
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark does not expose a session.commit() method in the same way; the correct pattern uses explicit BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK SQL statements.

options.text: Use Python try/except: execute all updates in try, call session.sql('ROLLBACK') in except, commit explicitly on success
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark stored procedures do not automatically wrap all statements in a single transaction. For explicit transaction control, use `session.sql('BEGIN')` at the start, execute all DML in a try block, call `session.sql('COMMIT')` on success, and `session.sql('ROLLBACK')` in the except block on failure. This is the standard pattern for atomic multi-statement operations.

options.text: Snowpark stored procedures automatically wrap all statements in a transaction; rollback occurs on unhandled exception
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark stored procedures do not automatically wrap operations in a transaction — explicit transaction management is the developer's responsibility.

questions.stem: A Snowpark Python script creates a session and runs a pipeline. After the pipeline completes, you notice the Snowflake warehouse is still running and billing. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The warehouse auto-suspend setting is too long; reduce it to 1 minute
options.explanation: is incorrect. The auto-suspend setting affects how long after inactivity the warehouse suspends, but the root cause here is the unclosed session.

options.text: The Snowpark session was not closed with session.close(), keeping the warehouse active
options.explanation: is correct. If `session.close()` is not called, the Snowpark session remains open. While the warehouse auto-suspends based on inactivity, an open session keeps the connection alive and can prevent or delay auto-suspend depending on the configuration. Best practice is to explicitly call `session.close()` (or use a context manager `with Session.builder.create() as session:`) to disconnect and allow immediate auto-suspend.

options.text: Snowflake warehouses cannot be auto-suspended when Snowpark sessions are active
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouses can auto-suspend even with Snowpark connections depending on the inactivity timer and whether there are pending queries.

options.text: Row-by-row execution outside a pushdown-friendly Snowpark expression prevents the warehouse from idling
options.explanation: is incorrect. Row-by-row execution can degrade performance but is not the cause of the warehouse remaining active after pipeline completion.

questions.stem: You must run a Snowpark Python application that uses a custom Docker image with GPU support for a deep learning workload. Which Snowflake service enables running containerized workloads within Snowflake?

options.text: Snowflake Snowsight Notebooks
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake Notebooks (Snowsight Notebooks) provide Python notebooks but without full Docker customization or GPU compute pools.

options.text: Snowpark Container Services
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark Container Services (SPCS) allows running custom Docker containers within Snowflake's infrastructure. Containers can access Snowflake data directly, and Snowflake provides compute pools including GPU-enabled instances for ML/AI workloads.

options.text: Snowflake External Functions
options.explanation: is incorrect. External Functions call external HTTPS endpoints outside Snowflake — they do not run containers inside Snowflake.

options.text: Snowflake Task with containerized jobs
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake Tasks execute SQL or stored procedure CALL statements on a schedule; they do not run Docker containers.

questions.stem: After training a model with Snowpark ML, you log it to the Snowflake Model Registry. How do you invoke the logged model for batch inference on a Snowpark DataFrame?

options.text: Deploy the model to a Snowpark Container Service endpoint first, then call the REST API
options.explanation: is incorrect. A Container Service endpoint is not required for batch inference on a DataFrame; the registry's run() method handles this directly.

options.text: Use CALL MODEL.PREDICT(table_name) SQL syntax
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no `CALL MODEL.PREDICT()` SQL syntax in Snowflake's Model Registry.

options.text: Export the model to pickle format and call it from a Python UDF
options.explanation: is incorrect. Exporting to pickle and calling from a UDF is a manual workaround that bypasses the Model Registry's built-in inference capabilities.

options.text: Retrieve the model with registry.get_model('model_name').default, then call model.run(df) to perform inference on the DataFrame
options.explanation: is correct. The Snowflake Model Registry allows retrieving logged models via `registry.get_model('name').version('v1')` (or `.default` for the default version). The retrieved model object exposes the method(s) registered during logging (typically `.run()` or `.predict()`). Calling `model.run(input_df)` executes inference entirely within Snowflake compute on the provided DataFrame, returning a result DataFrame with predictions.

questions.stem: A Snowpark job uses `session.createDataFrame()` to create a DataFrame from a local pandas DataFrame with 1 million rows. What performance concern exists with this approach in production?

options.text: createDataFrame() only works with Python primitive types; pandas DataFrames require .to_snowpark() instead
options.explanation: is incorrect. `createDataFrame()` does accept pandas DataFrames; there is no `.to_snowpark()` method.

options.text: createDataFrame() is limited to 10,000 rows
options.explanation: is incorrect. `createDataFrame()` is not limited to 10,000 rows, though larger datasets are increasingly inefficient.

options.text: No performance concern; createDataFrame() is optimized for large local datasets
options.explanation: is incorrect. `createDataFrame()` is not optimized for large local datasets — it is suitable for small test data or unit test fixtures only.

options.text: createDataFrame() uploads the local data to a Snowflake stage and inserts it into a temporary table, making it inefficient for large datasets compared to reading from a Snowflake source
options.explanation: is correct. `session.createDataFrame()` stages the local data (uploads it to a temporary Snowflake internal stage), inserts it into a temporary table, and creates a DataFrame pointing to that table. For large datasets, this upload + insert operation is slow and network-bound. In production, data should reside in Snowflake already and be accessed directly via `session.table()` or `session.read`.

questions.stem: Which Snowpark method allows you to execute an arbitrary SQL statement and return the result as a DataFrame?

options.text: session.sql(sql_string)
options.explanation: is correct. `session.sql(sql_string)` is the Snowpark method that creates a DataFrame representing the results of an arbitrary SQL query. Like other DataFrame operations, it is lazily evaluated — the SQL runs when an action (`.collect()`, `.show()`, `.to_pandas()`) is triggered.

options.text: session.execute(sql_string)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.execute()` is not a Snowpark Session method.

options.text: session.run(sql_string)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.run()` is not a Snowpark Session method.

options.text: session.query(sql_string)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.query()` is not a Snowpark Session method; this is a pandas/SQLAlchemy convention not present in Snowpark.

questions.stem: A Snowpark stored procedure creates a temporary table, populates it, runs analytics, and must ensure the temporary table is cleaned up even if an error occurs midway. How should you implement error handling?

options.text: Wrap all operations in a Snowflake transaction; rollback on error automatically drops the temporary table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Rolling back a transaction undoes DML changes but does NOT drop the temporary table — the table definition persists until session end or explicit DROP.

options.text: Use try/except/finally in Python: perform work in try, execute DROP TABLE IF EXISTS in finally to guarantee cleanup
options.explanation: is correct. Using `try/except/finally` in Python ensures the `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS` statement runs regardless of whether the procedure succeeds or fails. The `finally` block is guaranteed to execute, providing deterministic cleanup within the procedure's execution without waiting for session end.

options.text: Rely on session.close() to drop the temporary table; Snowflake cleans up on disconnect
options.explanation: is incorrect. While temporary tables are dropped when the session ends, waiting for session close is not a reliable cleanup strategy for long-running sessions — resources are not released immediately on error.

options.text: Register an atexit handler that executes the DROP TABLE statement when the procedure exits
options.explanation: is incorrect. `atexit` handlers are Python process-level hooks and do not translate to Snowflake stored procedure execution semantics.

questions.stem: Which Snowpark Python import provides access to Column functions like col(), lit(), and when()?

options.text: import snowflake.snowpark.functions as F
options.explanation: is also correct — `import snowflake.snowpark.functions as F` is another valid import that gives you `F.col()`, `F.lit()`, etc. However, option D is the more explicit form that directly imports the named functions.

options.text: from snowflake.snowpark.types import col, lit
options.explanation: is incorrect. `snowflake.snowpark.types` contains type classes (IntegerType, StringType, etc.), not column functions.

options.text: from snowflake.snowpark import col, lit
options.explanation: is incorrect. `col` and `lit` are not exported from the top-level `snowflake.snowpark` namespace; they are in `snowflake.snowpark.functions`.

options.text: from snowflake.snowpark.functions import col, lit, when
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark functions (`col`, `lit`, `sum`, `when`, etc.) are in the `snowflake.snowpark.functions` module. You can import specific functions directly with `from snowflake.snowpark.functions import col, lit, when`.

questions.stem: You want to unit test a Snowpark Python UDF locally before deploying it to Snowflake. The UDF contains pure Python logic with no Snowflake-specific dependencies. What is the simplest testing approach?

options.text: Extract the UDF handler function as a regular Python function and test it with pytest using Python unit tests, mocking any Snowflake-specific types
options.explanation: is correct. For UDFs with pure Python logic (no Snowflake-specific dependencies), the handler function is a standard Python function. You can test it directly with pytest by importing and calling the handler function with test inputs and asserting expected outputs. This is the fastest feedback loop — no Snowflake connection needed.

options.text: UDFs cannot be tested locally; Snowflake sandbox required
options.explanation: is incorrect. UDFs with pure Python logic can absolutely be tested locally without a Snowflake connection.

options.text: Deploy to a Snowflake development environment and test via SQL CALL
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deploying to a dev environment first adds unnecessary overhead for testing pure Python logic that requires no Snowflake-specific behavior.

options.text: Use the Snowflake Local Development Docker image to simulate the Snowflake execution environment
options.explanation: is incorrect. While a local development Docker image is useful for testing Snowpark DataFrame operations, it is heavier than needed for a pure Python function test.

questions.stem: A Snowpark DataFrame transformation produces incorrect results. You want to inspect the SQL that Snowpark generates without executing it. Which method should you use?

options.text: df.queries['queries']
options.explanation: is also a valid debugging approach. `df.queries['queries']` returns the raw SQL string(s) programmatically, which is useful for inspection. However, `explain()` is the more commonly used method for debugging query plans.

options.text: df.show_query()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.show_query()` is not a Snowpark DataFrame method.

options.text: df.to_sql()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.to_sql()` is a pandas method and does not exist on Snowpark DataFrames.

options.text: df.explain()
options.explanation: is correct. `df.explain()` prints the logical and physical query plan to stdout, showing how Snowpark translates the DataFrame transformations into SQL — without executing the query. It is the primary debugging tool for inspecting generated SQL.

questions.stem: A permanent Python UDF references a third-party library uploaded to '@my_pkg_stage/libs/mylib.zip'. After updating the library to a newer version and re-uploading to the same path, queries still use the old library version. Why?

options.text: Snowflake caches imported packages at the cluster level; clear the UDF cache by recreating the UDF
options.explanation: is partially correct but imprecise — the caching is at the warehouse worker level, not a separate 'cluster-level UDF cache'.

options.text: The stage file has a different ETag; Snowflake uses the original ETag for import validation
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not use ETags to validate imports in this way.

options.text: Overwriting a stage file does not update the UDF; you must use a new stage path and update the UDF's IMPORTS clause to reference the new path
options.explanation: is incorrect. Overwriting a stage file does update the physical file; the problem is that running warehouse workers have already cached the old file.

options.text: Snowflake warehouses cache imported stage files; a warehouse suspend/resume or UDF recreation is needed to pick up the new file
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake warehouse worker processes cache imported stage files for performance. When a stage file is overwritten (same path), active warehouse workers may continue using the cached version. Suspending and resuming the warehouse forces a cache flush, and workers reload the updated file on next use. Alternatively, recreating the UDF (`CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION`) also clears the cached import.

questions.stem: In Snowpark Python, which method triggers eager evaluation and returns all rows of a DataFrame as a list of Row objects to the client?

options.text: .collect()
options.explanation: is correct. `.collect()` is an action that executes the DataFrame's query plan against Snowflake and returns all resulting rows as a list of `snowflake.snowpark.Row` objects to the client.

options.text: .count()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `.count()` triggers execution but returns only the row count as an integer, not the rows themselves.

options.text: .show()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `.show()` triggers execution and prints a formatted preview (default 10 rows) but returns `None`, not the Row objects.

options.text: .to_pandas()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `.to_pandas()` triggers execution and returns a pandas DataFrame, not a list of Snowpark Row objects.

questions.stem: A Snowpark DataFrame `df` contains millions of rows. You must compute the 50th, 90th, and 99th percentiles of the `latency_ms` column. Which approach avoids pulling data to the client?

options.text: df.agg({'latency_ms': 'percentile'}).collect()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `'percentile'` is not a valid aggregate string in Snowpark's `.agg()` method.

options.text: df.to_pandas()['latency_ms'].quantile([0.5, 0.9, 0.99])
options.explanation: is incorrect. `.to_pandas()` pulls the entire DataFrame — millions of rows — to the client, which is extremely inefficient.

options.text: df.select(approx_percentile(col('latency_ms'), 0.5)).collect()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `approx_percentile()` computes only one approximate percentile value and does not compute all three in a single expression.

options.text: df.select(percentile_cont(0.5).within_group(col('latency_ms')), percentile_cont(0.9).within_group(col('latency_ms')), percentile_cont(0.99).within_group(col('latency_ms'))).collect()
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark exposes `percentile_cont()` with `within_group()` for exact percentile computation. This runs entirely within Snowflake — the SQL is pushed down and only the 3 scalar results are returned to the client via `.collect()`. No data is transferred.

questions.stem: A Snowpark pipeline must read from an external S3 stage, transform data, and write results to a Snowflake table. To avoid pulling data to the client and then re-uploading it, what is the correct approach?

options.text: Download files from S3 using boto3, transform locally, and upload to Snowflake via PUT
options.explanation: is incorrect. Downloading from S3 and re-uploading via PUT pulls data to the client, adding latency and egress costs.

options.text: Use session.file.get() to download files and process with pandas
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.file.get()` downloads the file to the local filesystem — the opposite of in-Snowflake processing.

options.text: Use session.sql('COPY INTO ...') to load data first, then query with session.table()
options.explanation: is incorrect. A COPY INTO loads data into a table but adds an extra step; chaining `session.read` directly allows transformations before the write.

options.text: Use session.read.csv(stage_path) to create a DataFrame from the stage, apply transformations, and use df.write.saveAsTable('target_table')
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark can read files directly from Snowflake stages (internal or external) using `session.read.csv()`, `session.read.json()`, etc. Chaining transformations and calling `df.write.saveAsTable()` generates a single SQL operation that reads from the stage and writes to the table entirely within Snowflake — no data is transferred to the client.

questions.stem: In Snowpark, what is the key difference between a UDF and a stored procedure?

options.text: UDFs can access external network; stored procedures are isolated to Snowflake
options.explanation: is incorrect. Both UDFs and stored procedures can be granted network egress permissions; this is not the key distinguishing difference.

options.text: Stored procedures are always faster than UDFs for the same logic
options.explanation: is incorrect. Performance depends on the implementation, not the object type.

options.text: UDFs support Python only; stored procedures support Python, Java, and JavaScript
options.explanation: is incorrect. Both UDFs and stored procedures support Python, Java, JavaScript, and Scala.

options.text: UDFs return a single value and can be called in SQL expressions; stored procedures can return any type and use CALL, and can manage sessions and transactions
options.explanation: is correct. The primary functional difference: UDFs (scalar UDFs, UDTFs) are called inline in SQL expressions (SELECT, WHERE, etc.) and return computed values per row or table. Stored procedures are called with CALL and can contain procedural logic, Snowpark operations, transaction management, and session manipulation.

questions.reference: [Choosing whether to write a stored procedure or a user-defined function](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/stored-procedures-vs-udfs)

questions.stem: You use Snowpark ML Modeling API to train a scikit-learn model entirely within Snowflake. What is the primary advantage over training the model locally with pandas?

options.text: Snowpark ML provides 10x faster training than scikit-learn on the same hardware
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark ML does not guarantee faster raw training speed than scikit-learn; the advantage is data locality, not raw speed.

options.text: Training executes on Snowflake compute within the data boundary; no data is transferred to the client, reducing latency and egress costs
options.explanation: is correct. The primary advantage of Snowpark ML Modeling is that training happens on Snowflake compute — data stays within Snowflake, there is no client-side data transfer (eliminates egress costs and latency), and you leverage Snowflake's scalable compute. The API mirrors scikit-learn (fit/predict/transform), so existing scikit-learn knowledge transfers directly.

options.text: Snowpark ML models are automatically deployed to Snowflake Model Registry without any additional steps
options.explanation: is incorrect. Models can be logged to the Snowflake Model Registry but this is a separate explicit step, not automatic.

options.text: Snowpark ML supports algorithms not available in scikit-learn
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark ML wraps scikit-learn algorithms; it does not add new algorithms beyond what scikit-learn provides.

questions.stem: When creating a Snowpark Python stored procedure, what does the `execute_as='caller'` parameter control?

options.text: The procedure executes SQL using the privileges of the procedure owner, not the calling user
options.explanation: is incorrect. This describes EXECUTE AS OWNER (the default), where the procedure runs with the procedure owner's privileges.

options.text: The procedure is called asynchronously by the user
options.explanation: is incorrect. `execute_as='caller'` controls privilege context, not synchronous vs. asynchronous execution.

options.text: The procedure runs in the caller's current session context (warehouse, database)
options.explanation: is incorrect. While caller's rights procedures do inherit some session context, the key distinction is the privilege set used for SQL authorization, not the warehouse or database context.

options.text: The procedure executes SQL using the privileges of the calling user, not the procedure owner
options.explanation: is correct. With `execute_as='caller'`, the procedure runs all SQL statements using the privilege set of the calling user. This is appropriate when the procedure should not have elevated privileges beyond the caller's own permissions.

questions.reference: [Understanding caller's rights and owner's rights stored procedures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/stored-procedure/stored-procedures-rights)

questions.stem: A Snowpark stored procedure is running in production and has a bug. You must hotfix it without disrupting queries currently calling the old version. Which Snowflake feature allows deploying a new version while keeping the old version available?

options.text: Use Snowflake Versioned Schema Objects (CREATE PROCEDURE with a VERSION) to register multiple versions and control which is the default
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake supports versioned stored procedures. Using `CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE` with versioning allows multiple versions to coexist. You can set a specific version as the default while keeping the old version available for existing calls.

options.text: Deploy the hotfix to a different schema and update callers to reference the new schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deploying to a different schema and updating callers requires code changes across all consumers and does not provide true versioning.

options.text: Use CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE which is atomic; in-flight calls complete before the replacement takes effect
options.explanation: is incorrect. `CREATE OR REPLACE` without versioning atomically replaces the procedure. In-flight calls may or may not complete depending on timing — this does not guarantee non-disruptive deployment.

options.text: Snowflake does not support versioned stored procedures; you must use a naming convention (procedure_v2) for hotfixes
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does support versioned stored procedures; a naming convention is a workaround, not a platform feature.

questions.stem: You want to schedule a Snowpark stored procedure to run every night at 2 AM UTC. Which Snowflake feature enables this without external scheduling?

options.text: Snowflake Resource Monitors
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource Monitors track compute costs and set usage limits; they do not schedule stored procedures.

options.text: Snowflake Pipes
options.explanation: is incorrect. Pipes are for continuous data loading via Snowpipe; they do not schedule stored procedure execution.

options.text: Snowflake Streams
options.explanation: is incorrect. Streams capture table change data (CDC); they do not provide scheduling. However, Streams are commonly paired with Tasks.

options.text: Snowflake Tasks
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake Tasks allow scheduling SQL statements or stored procedure CALL statements on a cron schedule or at a fixed interval. A task defined with `SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 2 * * * UTC'` would call the stored procedure every night at 2 AM UTC without external schedulers.

questions.stem: You want to log an ML model trained with Snowpark ML to the Snowflake Model Registry, including metadata about training dataset version and evaluation metrics. Which Model Registry method enables this?

options.text: trained_model.save_to_registry(registry, version='v1')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `trained_model.save_to_registry()` is not a method on Snowpark ML model objects.

options.text: registry.create_model('model_name', model=trained_model, metrics={'rmse': 0.05}, comment='trained on v2 dataset')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `registry.create_model()` is not the correct method name; the API uses `log_model()`.

options.text: registry.log_model(trained_model, model_name='model_name', version_name='v1', metrics={'rmse': 0.05}, comment='trained on v2 dataset')
options.explanation: is correct. The Snowflake Model Registry's `log_model()` method is the primary API for logging a trained model. It accepts the model object, a model name, version name, and optional metadata including metrics dictionaries and comments/tags.

options.text: registry.save(trained_model, 'model_name', metadata={'rmse': 0.05})
options.explanation: is incorrect. `registry.save()` is not a valid Model Registry method.

questions.stem: A Snowpark DataFrame operation must write results to an existing Snowflake table, adding new rows without overwriting existing data. Which write mode should you use?

options.text: df.write.mode('merge').saveAsTable('target_table')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `'merge'` is not a standard Snowpark write mode — MERGE operations require using `session.sql()` with a MERGE INTO statement or Snowpark's `merge()` method.

options.text: df.write.mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('target_table')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `'overwrite'` truncates the table and replaces all existing data with the DataFrame's data.

options.text: df.write.mode('append').saveAsTable('target_table')
options.explanation: is correct. The `'append'` write mode inserts rows from the DataFrame into the existing table without truncating or replacing existing data.

options.text: df.write.mode('ignore').saveAsTable('target_table')
options.explanation: is incorrect. `'ignore'` skips writing if the table exists — no error is raised and no data is written.

questions.stem: An engineer creates a Snowpark DataFrame using `session.table('SALES')`. No query has been submitted yet. Which characteristic describes this DataFrame?

options.text: The DataFrame is eagerly evaluated; Snowflake executes the query immediately on creation
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowpark DataFrames are lazily evaluated, not eagerly evaluated.

options.text: The data has been fully loaded into the client's memory
options.explanation: is incorrect. No data is loaded into client memory until an action like `.collect()` or `.to_pandas()` is called.

options.text: The DataFrame is lazily evaluated; it represents the query plan but has not executed against Snowflake
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark DataFrames use lazy evaluation (deferred execution). Creating a DataFrame with `session.table()` or applying transformations (`.filter()`, `.select()`, etc.) builds a logical query plan without sending any SQL to Snowflake. The query is only executed when an action is triggered (e.g., `.collect()`, `.show()`, `.write.saveAsTable()`).

options.text: The DataFrame creates a temporary table in Snowflake on creation
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.table()` creates a DataFrame referencing the existing table; it does not create a new temporary table.

questions.stem: A Snowpark stored procedure must call another stored procedure and use its return value. In Snowpark Python, how do you call a stored procedure and capture its return value?

options.text: result = session.call('procedure_name', arg1, arg2)
options.explanation: is correct. `session.call()` is the idiomatic Snowpark approach for calling a stored procedure and returning its result directly. It executes the procedure within the same session/transaction context.

options.text: result = session.sql('CALL procedure_name(arg1, arg2)').collect()[0][0]
options.explanation: is also a valid approach — `session.sql('CALL ...').collect()[0][0]` executes the CALL statement as raw SQL and extracts the return value from the resulting Row. However, `session.call()` is the preferred Snowpark idiom.

options.text: stored_proc_obj = session.call('procedure_name', arg1, arg2); result = stored_proc_obj.result()
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.call()` returns the result value directly, not an object with a `.result()` method.

options.text: result = session.execute_procedure('procedure_name', arg1, arg2)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `session.execute_procedure()` is not a Snowpark Session method.

questions.stem: A Python UDF processes text documents using an NLP model loaded from a file. Loading the model takes 10 seconds. The UDF is called millions of times per query. How do you avoid reloading the model on every UDF call?

options.text: Use a module-level (global) variable to load the model once when the UDF handler module is first imported; subsequent calls reuse the cached model from the global variable
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake UDF worker processes persist between calls within a session/query, meaning module-level state is preserved. A model loaded into a module-level variable is loaded once when the worker first handles a call and reused for subsequent calls in the same worker process. This is the recommended pattern for expensive model loading in Snowflake Python UDFs.

options.text: Load the model in the __init__ method of a class-based UDF handler
options.explanation: is also a valid pattern — loading in `__init__` of a class-based handler achieves the same per-worker caching behavior. However, the module-level variable approach (option A) is the most straightforward.

options.text: Store the model in Snowflake as a VARIANT column and load it with a SELECT query inside the UDF
options.explanation: is incorrect. Storing the model as a VARIANT and querying it from inside the UDF on every call would be extremely slow and counterproductive.

options.text: Use UDF caching by setting RESULT_SCAN_CACHE=TRUE on the function
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no `RESULT_SCAN_CACHE=TRUE` parameter for UDFs. Result caching applies to queries, not to internal UDF state.

questions.stem: You must apply a custom Python function element-wise to each row of a Snowpark DataFrame column where the function requires pandas operations. Which Snowpark approach allows this without writing a full UDF deployment?

options.text: df.apply(python_func, axis=1)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.apply()` is a pandas method and does not exist on Snowpark DataFrames.

options.text: Use session.udf.register() to register an anonymous UDF and apply it with col() in withColumn()
options.explanation: is correct. `session.udf.register()` registers a Python function as a temporary UDF within the current session without needing a separate CREATE FUNCTION DDL statement. The registered function returns a Snowpark Column object that can be used in `withColumn()`, `select()`, and other DataFrame transformations.

options.text: Convert to pandas with .to_pandas(), apply the function, then create a new Snowpark DataFrame
options.explanation: is incorrect. Converting to pandas pulls all data to the client and then requires re-uploading — this defeats the purpose of server-side processing.

options.text: Use pandas_udf with the @pandas_udf decorator in Snowpark
options.explanation: is incorrect. `@pandas_udf` is a PySpark concept; Snowpark does not have a `pandas_udf` decorator. Snowpark does support vectorized UDFs, but via a different mechanism.

questions.stem: You have two Snowpark DataFrames `orders` and `customers`. You want to join them on `customer_id` and keep only rows where a matching customer exists. Which join type is correct?

options.text: orders.join(customers, orders['customer_id'] == customers['customer_id'], 'left')
options.explanation: is incorrect. A left join keeps all orders (including those with no matching customer, where customer columns will be null).

options.text: orders.crossJoin(customers).filter(orders['customer_id'] == customers['customer_id'])
options.explanation: is incorrect. Cross join + filter is semantically equivalent to inner join but extremely inefficient, generating M×N rows before filtering.

options.text: orders.join(customers, orders['customer_id'] == customers['customer_id'], 'outer')
options.explanation: is incorrect. An outer (full outer) join keeps all rows from both sides, with nulls where there is no match.

options.text: orders.join(customers, orders['customer_id'] == customers['customer_id'], 'inner')
options.explanation: is correct. An inner join returns only rows where the join condition is satisfied in both DataFrames — only orders with a matching customer are retained.

questions.stem: A Snowpark DataFrame `df` has columns [customer_id, order_date, amount]. You must add a column `amount_usd` that converts `amount` (in GBP) by multiplying by 1.27. Which Snowpark expression is correct?

options.text: df.add_column('amount_usd', col('amount') * 1.27)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.add_column()` is not a Snowpark DataFrame method.

options.text: df.withColumn('amount_usd', df['amount'] * 1.27)
options.explanation: is correct. `withColumn(name, expr)` adds a new column or replaces an existing one — this is the most readable and idiomatic Snowpark approach for adding a derived column.

options.text: df.mutate(amount_usd=col('amount') * 1.27)
options.explanation: is incorrect. `df.mutate()` is a dplyr/tidyverse (R) convention; it does not exist in Snowpark.

options.text: df.select('*', (col('amount') * 1.27).alias('amount_usd'))
options.explanation: is also a valid approach — `df.select('*', expr.alias('name'))` selects all existing columns plus the new derived column. However, `withColumn` (option B) is the cleaner idiom for a single column addition.

questions.stem: You create a Snowpark Python UDF that calculates a custom risk score from three numeric columns. The function is deterministic. Which UDF characteristic improves query performance for this function?

options.text: Define the UDF as IMMUTABLE (using is_permanent=True and immutable=True) to allow Snowflake to cache and reuse results for identical inputs
options.explanation: is correct. Marking a UDF as IMMUTABLE tells Snowflake that the function always returns the same result for the same inputs (deterministic, no side effects). Snowflake can then cache the result for repeated calls with identical arguments, avoiding redundant Python process invocations.

options.text: Use a Scala UDF instead of Python for better performance
options.explanation: is incorrect. While Scala UDFs may have lower per-invocation overhead than Python UDFs, the question asks about a characteristic of the UDF definition that improves performance, not a language switch.

options.text: Deploy the UDF to a dedicated compute pool to run independently
options.explanation: is incorrect. Deploying to a dedicated compute pool applies to Snowpark Container Services, not to scalar UDFs.

options.text: Define the UDF as VOLATILE to prevent Snowflake from caching results
options.explanation: is incorrect. VOLATILE means the function can return different values for the same inputs, which disables result caching and degrades performance for a deterministic function.

questions.stem: A Snowpark Python UDF is registered as a permanent UDF in schema MY_SCHEMA. A query in a different schema needs to call it. What is the fully qualified syntax?

options.text: SELECT DATABASE.MY_SCHEMA.my_udf(column_name) FROM table_name
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake uses three-part naming for database objects: `DATABASE.SCHEMA.OBJECT_NAME`. To call a permanent UDF from a different schema or database, use the fully qualified name `DATABASE.MY_SCHEMA.my_udf()`.

options.text: SELECT MY_SCHEMA.my_udf(column_name) FROM table_name
options.explanation: is partially correct — `MY_SCHEMA.my_udf()` works when the caller is in the same database, but the fully qualified form with the database name is the explicit, unambiguous syntax.

options.text: UDFs cannot be called from schemas other than the one they are registered in
options.explanation: is incorrect. Permanent UDFs can be called from any schema, subject to GRANT USAGE privileges on the schema and GRANT USAGE on the function.

options.text: CALL MY_SCHEMA.my_udf(column_name)
options.explanation: is incorrect. CALL is used for stored procedures, not scalar UDFs. Scalar UDFs are called in a SELECT expression.

questions.stem: A Snowpark Container Services application needs to connect back to Snowflake to query data. Which method provides secure, credential-free connectivity from the container to Snowflake?

options.text: Use the Snowflake JDBC driver with OAuth2 token from AWS IAM role federation
options.explanation: is incorrect. AWS IAM role federation is an external identity mechanism and is not the built-in SPCS authentication pattern.

options.text: Generate a temporary Snowflake API key and pass it at container startup
options.explanation: is incorrect. Generating and passing a temporary API key at startup adds operational complexity and is not the SPCS-native approach.

options.text: Store Snowflake credentials in the container's environment variables using Docker SECRETS
options.explanation: is incorrect. Storing credentials in Docker environment variables is a security risk and is not necessary given the built-in SNOWFLAKE_TOKEN injection.

options.text: Use the SNOWFLAKE_TOKEN environment variable automatically injected by SPCS and the Snowflake Python connector with token-based authentication
options.explanation: is correct. Snowpark Container Services automatically injects a `SNOWFLAKE_TOKEN` environment variable into running containers. This token is a short-lived OAuth token scoped to the service's Snowflake role. The Snowflake Python connector (and other drivers) can authenticate using this token without storing long-lived credentials in the container or image.

questions.stem: You use Snowpark ML's Feature Store to register features computed from raw event data. Which key benefit does the Feature Store provide?

options.text: Features are pre-computed and cached in a separate table for all possible entities
options.explanation: is incorrect. Feature values are not necessarily pre-computed for all entities; they can be computed on-demand during retrieval based on the stored definition.

options.text: Feature definitions are stored as SQL/Python code; training and serving pipelines use the same feature computation, preventing training-serving skew
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's Feature Store stores feature definitions (as Python/SQL logic) and ensures that the same computation is used for both training data generation and online/batch serving. This eliminates training-serving skew — a common ML production issue where training uses different feature logic than serving.

options.text: The Feature Store encrypts all feature values at rest automatically
options.explanation: is incorrect. Encryption is handled by Snowflake's standard data encryption; the Feature Store does not add a separate encryption layer.

options.text: Feature pipelines run on a dedicated Snowflake compute pool separate from the main warehouse
options.explanation: is incorrect. Feature pipelines run on standard Snowflake warehouses, not a separate dedicated compute pool.

questions.stem: A Python UDTF processes large batches of input rows to generate output rows. Performance is slow due to Python's row-by-row overhead. Which UDTF feature allows processing batches of rows as pandas DataFrames for vectorized operations?

options.text: Using the vectorized UDTF interface where the process() method receives a pandas DataFrame instead of individual rows
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake supports vectorized Python UDTFs where the `process()` method signature accepts a pandas DataFrame (receiving a batch of input rows) instead of individual scalar arguments. This enables vectorized operations using pandas/numpy, dramatically improving throughput compared to row-by-row Python processing.

options.text: Switching to a Scala UDTF which automatically vectorizes
options.explanation: is incorrect. Scala UDTFs have lower per-row overhead than Python but do not 'automatically vectorize' in the same pandas-batch sense.

options.text: Enabling PARALLEL_EXECUTION on the UDTF
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no `PARALLEL_EXECUTION` parameter for UDTFs in Snowflake.

options.text: Setting batch_size parameter in the UDTF CREATE statement
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no `batch_size` parameter in the UDTF DDL; the vectorized interface is declared via the method signature type hints.
